— a

—

i

Page 4 — THE TRIBUNE, Elkin-Jonesville, N.C., Wednesday, October 23, 1974

Guitarist plans music series

By MARY NORMAN Special To The Tribune

The public is invtied to a special program of music, Sunday afternoon at the Elkin Public Library. Al Kunze, classic guitarist of Surry Community College, will play selections on his guitar.

Mr. Kunze is one of 40 visiting artists at local community colleges throughout the state for the purpose of correlating var- ious art forms with all aspects of education.

This group represents many art forms. Indivi- duals are chosen by the State Department of Edu- cation after careful screen- ing and placed in an area best suited to take advan- tage of each artist's special skills and talents.

The program is a joint

effort on the part of com- munity colleges and the Department of Education to undergrid the overall educational structure. Each artist will visit all schools in the college area and also other strategic places such as libraries and community centers.

The program Sunday afternoon scheduled from 3-5, is the first in a series of three such programs to be held at the Elkin Library throughout the school year. Prior to each program, Mr. Kunze will visit area schools and have sessions with the children in groups of around 50 for the purpose of acquainting them with the classical guitar rendi- tions.

Create Interest

Mr. Kunze says that his chief function is to create

an interest in classical music that can be played on an instrument usually associated with pop,-coun- try or modern music. How- ever, one has to be able to read music in order to play classic music on the guitar.

At the program on Sun- day, some background in- formation concerning each composition will be given. An informal atmosphere is maintained throughout each performance to en- able the audience to just relax and enjoy the music.

Mr. Kunze held a similar post at Mayland Institute at Spruce Pine last year. He has a B.A. degree in music history and litera- ture from the University of Florida and a master of music in guitar from Flori- da State University, the latter under a full graduate

ee eee p——

Al Kunze, classic guitarist, will give a program of music at the Elkin Public Library, Sunday afternoon.

fellowship.

He has also studied with the British composer- teacher, John W. Duarte and Argentinian virtuoso Manuel Lopez Ramos. He is a member of the Ameri- can Musicological Society, the Society of the Classic

Guitar and the honorary societies, Pi Kappa Lamb- da and Phi Beta Kappa.

A similar program will be held at the Yadkin County Library, Sunday afternoon, Nov. 3, from 3-5 p.m.

From Completing Education

True grit, determination, tenacity, resolution—call it what you will. An area youth has something that drives him on- ward despite the fickle finger of fate, which deprived him of ‘mobility and confined him to a

* wheelchair. $

Richard Watts, son of Mr. and Mrs. Robert L. Watts, route 8, Mount Airy, has spent six years in a wheelchair since a fateful accident, at the age of 16, which left him incapicated.

The accident occurred in a dive from a tree into the Ararat River off Riverside Drive. Richard hit the bottom and sustained a broken neck.

For seven months, he was in and out of Baptist and Forsyth Memorial Hospital in Winston- Salem and Northern Surry Hospital. He eventually recovered enough to be discharged, but with the realization that he would be in a wheelchair.

Just recently, Richard celebrated his 23rd birthday. His birthday found him involved in working at becoming an ac- countant.

Young Watts is currently studying accounting at home through the Surry Community College at Dobson.

Each Tuesday morning, for an hour, Pat Barfield, accounting instructor at the college, makes the trip to Richard's home to make a check on his progress, help him with any problem he might have with the course, and make necessary assignments of study.

Watts began his studies with the college at the beginning of the fall quarter. He has 15 months (two quarters) of study in ac- counting with the Woodrow Wilson Rehabilitation Center at Fisherville, Va., from May 1972 through August 1973.

After coming home from the rehabilitation center, time began to hang heavily on his hands. A mutual friend, taking an evening class in accounting at the college, brought to Barfield’s attention

that Richard had been studying

accounting but was no longer doing so.

After ascertaining that Richard would be interested in pursuing his studies at home. Barfield consulted with James H. Wallace, chairman of the college business department. Wallace

.gave his approval. All of Watts’

credits were transferred to Surry Community College.

Barfield said Richard is a good student.

“‘When he comes to a problem he can’t solve, Richard is sup- posed to contact us by telephone, but he is doing so well, he doesn’t

—The Mount Airy News. Friday, November 1, 1974.

ever bother to call,” Barfield said.

“This is the first time we've done this type thing—it’s more or less in the coordinating stage— but we’d like to do more of it,” Barfield affirmed.

Barfield said Watts, with his background study, could easily work on to obtain an Associate Science Degree in business ad- ministration.

James M. Reeves, dean of students at the college, said, “Students usually have to attend

classes for instruction, but we have many courses set up where

...Barfield assists Watts with a question which needed an answer... —staff photo

‘Wheelchair Doesn’t Confine Watts

students in situations similar to Watts’ may take a course at home.

“This emphasizes the flexibility of our curriculum, which allows us to do this. And the beauty of it is that we have instructors, like Barfield, who are willing to go out and spend time with people like Watts. If Watts has the determination to get a degree, we're going to see that he gets enough courses to his home to allow him to get it. It's the philosophy of Surry Com- munity College, and all com- munity colleges throughout N.C., to assist persons interested in securing more education,” Reeves said.

Reeves pointed out that the element of time would not allow but a few persons at a time to receive instruction at home. © Though he is now studying accounting, Watts, who was a sophomore at North Surry High School at the time of his accident, said he would probably have to go into the construction field after graduation.

For relaxation, he likes to fish, target shoot and watch football and baseball on television.

Th

Haywood Merritt,.chairman of the Surry County Commissioners, and Dr. James A. Harrell Sr. of Elkin display the

county’s official Bicentennial flag, pre- sented by Mr. Max Dixon of the State Bicentennial Commission.

Bicentennial status conferred on county

DOBSON — Surry County officially became a Bicentennial Community Sun- day at Surry Community College here

Sunday after- —_———————n noon. Rep. Wilmer | Surry ay

Mizell, Fifth Dis- trict Congressman, told about 200 guests that, “For 200 years, generations of Americans here in Surry County and throughout this land have done the climb- ing, the reaching and the striving for a better country.”

Surry County, the 10th community in the state to become an official Bicenten- nial Community, was congratulated by the incumbent congressman for ‘the high honor that reflects the best of history and promises even higher achievement in a challenging future.”

The audience, many attired in red,

e Tribune, Elkin-Jonesville, N.C., Friday, November 8, 1974 Surry Community to offer adult program

DOBSON — Surry Com- munity College will offer a special program beginning winter quarter for adults who wish to start or to return to college.

The program is designed to enable adults to begin or resume their college ca- reers in association with other adult students. An objective of the program is to allow adults to try various academic areas and to aid them in match- ing their abilities to col- lege-level work.

Courses will be held on

ly showing their latest creations. Miss Sykes is the daughter Elsie Sykes, route six, Mount Airy. She graduated from y High School and Surry Community College.

s of Los Angeles, California and travels extensively with the

ESSIONAL MODEL—Miss Pamela Jean Sykes is shown ing at a show at Dallas, Texas. She is employed by Funky

the college campus begin- ning Nov. 25. Completion of courses will provide col- lege credit which may be used toward a degree at SCC. transferred to an- other college. or used for an individual's self-im- provement.

Cost

The total cost of classes will depend upon the num- ber of courses taken.

Registration for the pro- gram will be held at SCC Nov. 25 at 9:30 a.m. In-

‘terested persons are en-

couraged to call the college at 386-8121 to place their name on the roll or to receive additional informa- tion.

white and blue, were primarily political officeholders, candidates (such as Rep. Mizell’s Democratic opponent, Steve Neal), members of the Bicentennial committees, officials’ relatives or the North Surry High School band and Surry Central color guard.

George Marion Jr., Democratic candi- date for the state senate, in remarks preceeding Rep. Mizell’s, said, “I believe that if it could be possible for some of our forefathers to be with us today their hearts would swell with pride just as ours - are doing here today as we begin to celebrate this great occasion.”

Certificate And Flag

Haywood Merritt, chairman of the Surry Commissioners, accepted the offi- cial Bicentennial certificate and flag from Dr. Max Dixon of the State Bicentennial Commission.

Ed Spanski, executive director of the Surry Bicentennial Commission, an- nounced final details for a Surry County flag and seal contest.

All residents of Surry County are eligible to submit designs for a county flag and seal. In each contest, first place prize money will be $50, second place $25 and third place $10 — given by the Lion’s Club of Dobson.

Entries must be received by Ed Spanski, P.O. Box 1776, Dobson, N.C., 27017, by Dec. 1, with a screening committee choosing 10 finalists in the seal and flag contests to be submitted to the county commissioners for a final decision. ee
